coredata.dc:title: Pyrrolic nitrogen dominated the carbon dot mimic oxidase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huibo
Zhang, Mengling
Wei, Kaiqiang
Zhao, Yajie
Nie, Haodong
Ma, Yurong
Zhou, Yunjie
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Shao, Mingwang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with enzyme-like activities play crucial roles in nanobiotechnology. However, it is difficult to establish a relationship between their structure and biocatalytic properties. In this work, a series of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (TA-NCDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Their oxidase-like activity could be triggered by visible light irradiation (420 nm≤λ ≤ 700 nm). As a nanozyme, TA-NCDs show the value of maximum reaction rates (Vmax) is 121.95 × 10−8 M/s and the Michaelis–Menten constants (Km) is 0.61 mM. In situ transient photovoltage (TPV) tests prove that the pyrrolic nitrogen in TA-NCDs is the catalytic active sites and substrate binding sites. Under visible light irradiation, TA-NCDs, with oxidase-like activity, increased the cellular ROS level significantly, leading to their visible light induced bioactivity to kill nearly 60% of HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxidase-like activity
Pyrrolic nitrogen
Visible light
Nanozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetic assay of visible light-driven oxidase-like activity
Stability test of TA-NCDs as nanozymes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China ( 2018YFE0306105 ), National Key R&D Program of China ( 2020YFA0406104 ), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51972216 , 52041202 ‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190041 ), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province ( 2019B010933001 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , and the 111 Project .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of TA-NCDs. (a) A schematic fabrication of TA-NCDs. (b) TEM image of TA-NCDs-3 (the bottom inset: size distribution diagrams, the top-right inset: the HRTEM image). (c) UV–vis absorption and e FTIR spectra of TA-NCDs-3, tartaric acid, and 3-aminophenol. (d) The excitation-emission fluorescence spectra of TA-NCDs-3. (f) C1s and g N 1s XPS spectra of TA-NCDs-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Band structure of TA-NCDs. (a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum and (b) corresponding Tauc plot of TA-NCDs-3. (c) CV curve of TA-NCDs-3 modified GC electrode in the nitrogen-saturated Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (0.1 M) with ferrocene as internal standard. (d) Band structure diagram from TA-NCDs-1 to TA-NCDs-5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Enzyme kinetics of TA-NCDs. (a) Schematic diagram of TMB oxidation by TA-NCDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of catalytic reaction in visible light and darkness. (c) Curves of the absorbance at 652 nm of catalytic reaction systems against time. (d) Kinetics for oxidase-like activity of TA-NCDs-1 to TA-NCDs-5. (e) Lineweaver-Burk Plot of TA-NCDs-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Enzymatic stability of TA-NCDs-3 in different conditions. (a) pH values, (b) temperatures, (c) ionic strengths, and (d) various metallic salt solutions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Catalytic mechanism of TA-NCDs-3. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of reaction systems saturated with air, N2 and O2. (b) Comparison of TMB oxidation catalyzed by TA-NCDs-3 in different scavengers. (c, d) DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of TA-NCDs-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Elucidation of the oxidase-like activity origin of TA-NCDs-3. (a) The TPV intensities of TA-NCDs-3 at different atmospheres (N2 and O2). (b) The TPV intensities of TA-NCDs-3 in the presence of TMB at N2 and O2 atmospheres. (c) Relationship between pyrrolic nitrogen in TA-NCDs and oxygen activation. (d) Relationship between pyrrolic nitrogen of TA-NCDs and Vmax values. (e) Mechanism diagram for TMB oxidation catalyzed by TA-NCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cellular localization of TA-NCDs in HeLa and its effects on viability. (a) Cellular localization of TA-NCDs in HeLa cells and plot analysis of region of interest (ROI) across HeLa cells (green line: labeled by TA-NCDs, red line: labeled by MitoTracker® Deep Red FM or RedDot™ 1). Scale bar: 20 μm. (b) Effects of TA-NCDs on HeLa cell viability. (c) Normalized cellular ROS level in HeLa cells after TA-NCDs stimulation. (d) Schematic for TA-NCDs induced tumor cell destruction. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Relative contents of nitrogen-doped forms (pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and graphitic N) in TA-NCDs.

head.ce:title: Pyrrolic nitrogen dominated the carbon dot mimic oxidase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou, 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with enzyme-like activities play crucial roles in nanobiotechnology. However, it is difficult to establish a relationship between their structure and biocatalytic properties. In this work, a series of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (TA-NCDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Their oxidase-like activity could be triggered by visible light irradiation (420 nm≤λ ≤ 700 nm). As a nanozyme, TA-NCDs show the value of maximum reaction rates (Vmax) is 121.95 × 10−8 M/s and the Michaelis–Menten constants (Km) is 0.61 mM. In situ transient photovoltage (TPV) tests prove that the pyrrolic nitrogen in TA-NCDs is the catalytic active sites and substrate binding sites. Under visible light irradiation, TA-NCDs, with oxidase-like activity, increased the cellular ROS level significantly, leading to their visible light induced bioactivity to kill nearly 60% of HeLa cells.

Pyrrolic nitrogen dominated the mimic oxidase activity of CDs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial enzymes have attracted much attention in many fields, such as enzyme-based diagnosis, cancer therapy and new energy, due to their excellent catalytic performances. Nanomaterials with high catalytic activity and uncomplicated synthetic process are entitled as nanozymes and considered as the most likely alternative for natural enzymes. Up to now, a series of nanomaterials were utilized to develop nanozymes, including Fe3O4, CeO2, V2O5, Au, Pt nanoparticles, metal–organic framework (MOF), and graphene, etc. [1] For these nanozymes, variety of mimic enzyme activities were demonstrated, such as mimics of peroxidase, oxidase, catalase, superoxide dismutase [1]. However, there are still remain many challenges, such as uncontrollable activity, unclear catalytic mechanism and unclear structure-activity relationship. Carbon dots (CDs), with high catalytic activity and biocompatibility, have been applied in many fields such as photo-electro-catalysis [2], photoluminescence [3], nanozymes [4–10] and disease therapy [11]. Recently, CDs derived artificial enzymes including peroxidase-like [4,5], oxidase-like [6,7], antioxidant enzyme-like [9] and DNA endonuclease-like nanozymes [10] were reported. However, it is a great challenge to clarify the relationship between their structures and enzyme-like activities, which is the key step to develop CDs-based nanozymes and expand their bio-applications.

Here, we fabricated a series of nitrogen-doped CDs (TA-NCDs) with different contents of pyrrolic nitrogen from tartaric acid and 3-aminophenol by hydrothermal method. TA-NCDs show a high catalytic activity triggered by visible light irradiation (420 nm≤λ ≤ 700 nm), by which the TA-NCDs are able to oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with mimic oxidase catalysis behavior. In these experiments, TA-NCDs show excellent catalytic activity, and the value of maximum reaction rates (Vmax) is 121.95 × 10−8 M/s and the Michaelis constant (Km) is 0.61 mM. With the help of scavengers and electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR), it is confirmed that the oxidization of TMB is mediated by superoxide radicals (•O2 −), which are generated from the oxygen activation by TA-NCDs. In situ TPV experiments further prove that the pyrrolic nitrogen of TA-NCDs is both the catalytic active sites and substrate binding sites. Furthermore, the applicability of TA-NCDs in bio-systems was verified, in which, TA-NCDs are capable of enriching in mitochondria and killing nearly 60% of HeLa cells under visible light irradiation.

TA-NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using tartaric acid and 3-aminophenol as precursors. Briefly, tartaric acid and 3-aminophenol with a total mass of 2 g in different molar ratios (4 : 1, 1 : 2, 1 : 1, 2 : 1,1 : 4) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, then each of them was transferred into a 15 mL Teflon lined stainless steel high-pressure hydrothermal tank and heat at 160 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, these solutions were filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous filter to remove insoluble impurities, and dialyzed against a 500–1000 Da cellulose dialysis bag to remove unreacted precursor. Finally, five kinds of solutions with different reactant ratios were obtained, which were entitled as TA-NCDs-1, TA-NCDs-2, TA-NCDs-3, TA-NCDs-4 and TA-NCDs-5, respectively.

3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was chosen as a substrate and the absorbance at 652 nm (Abs 652) of TMB oxidation product was used to evaluate the mimic oxidase activity of TA-NCDs. The reaction system contained: TA-NCDs (final concentration: 100 μg/mL), TMB in DMSO (final concentration: 0–1 mM) and NaAc–HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 4). Subsequently, the reaction system was irradiated with visible light (420 nm≤λ ≤ 700 nm) with an average intensity of 30 W/cm2 and an irradiation area of 9.0746 cm2. Meanwhile, the reaction system placed under dark condition was set as the control group. The Abs 652 of the reaction system was measured with a multifunctional microplate reader. The experiment was performed three times to ensure the accuracy of the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic assay of visible light-driven oxidase-like activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The kinetic processes of TA-NCDs-1 to TA-NCDs-5 were measured by recording the Abs 652 of the oxidation products of TMB. The relationship between the enzymatic reaction velocity and substrate concentration was fitted to the Michaelis Menten equation, which was a classical model of the kinetics of the enzymatic reaction. Michaelis Menten equation (Eq. (1)) was shown as follow:
(Eq. 1)
V =
[ S ]
×
V
m a x
[ S ]
+
K m
where Vmax was the maximum reaction velocity, reflecting the catalytic activity, and Km was the Michaelis constant, reflecting the affinity for substrates. Vmax and Km values could be plotted by Lineweaver Burk's double reciprocal plotting (Eq. (2)):
(Eq. 2)
1 V
=
K m
V
m a x
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V
m a x

By plotting the linear fit of the reciprocal of the reaction rate (1/V) against the reciprocal of the substrate concentration (1/[S]), the Vmax and Km values were calculated according to the slope and intercept.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability test of TA-NCDs as nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: TA-NCDs-3 solution (100 μg/mL), TMB solution (1 mM) were mixed under different conditions, including pH values (4–8), temperatures (4 °C–85 °C), ionic strengths (0 M–1 M NaCl) and metallic salt solutions (100 μM MgCl2, 100 μM AlCl3, 100 μM CaCl2, 100 μM MnCl2, 100 μM FeCl3, 100 μM CoCl2, 100 μM NiCl2, 100 μM CuCl2 and 100 μM ZnCl2). After irradiation with visible light for 1 min, the Abs 652 was recorded using a microplate reader.

HeLa cells (1 × 104 cells) were seeded into a confocal dish. After incubation of 24 h, TA-NCDs (100 μg/mL, Ex/Em = 488/516 nm) were added into confocal dishes for 30 min, respectively. After washing the cells with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) for three times, TA-NCDs treated cells were stained with commercial organelle probes including MitoTracker® Deep Red FM (Ex/Em = 644/665 nm) and RedDot™ 1 (Ex/Em = 647/665 nm), according to the products’ instructions, respectively. After washing the cells with PBS buffer for three times, confocal images of these cells were recorded with a confocal microscope (LSM 800 Zeiss) and collected with a 63 × oil-immersionobjective. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the line in the confocal images was analysed by using Zeiss software.

HeLa cells were chosen as a cell model to assess the cell viability of TA-NCDs. Briefly, HeLa cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were cultivated in a 96-well plate with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle (DMEM) medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. Then, the plate was transferred to a cell incubator filled with 5% of CO2 at 37 °C. After 24 h, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing different concentrations of TA-NCDs (0–250 μg/mL) and cultured under visible light and darkness for 1 h, respectively. Then, cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was used to assess the cytotoxicity according to the absorbance at 450 nm.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of TA-NCDs (black line) show two obvious absorption bands of located at 258 nm and 385 nm, which are assigned to the typical absorption bands of π-π∗ transition and n-π∗ transition (Fig. 1c and S2) [14]. Compared with 3-aminophenol (red line), TA-NCDs have red-shifted absorption bands, which may be caused by the formation of smaller electronic band gap [15]. The absorption band at nearly 490 nm of TA-NCDs-3 should be attributed to the exciton transition [14]. TA-NCDs can emit yellow fluorescence under 365 nm of UV light (the inset of Fig. 1c). When TA-NCDs are excited by light from 300 to 500 nm, these TA-NCDs emit strong fluorescence from 500 to 700 nm (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence excitation-emission spectra suggest TA-NCDs-3 have an independent-excitation fluorescence property. Further tests (Fig. S4) also show that the fluorescence signal intensities of TA-NCDs-3 are stable at acid conditions, different temperatures (25–85 °C), as well as ionic strengths (0–1 M NaCl), and in various types of metallic salt solutions.

Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra of TA-NCDs were recorded to analyze their chemical functional groups, elemental compositions, and structure. As shown in Fig. 1e and S5, a broad band from 3000 to 3600 cm−1 of TA-NCDs-3 (black line) is assigned as O–H and N–H bonds [16]. Considering that TA-NCDs-3 and tartaric acid (blue line) both show two peaks at 1729 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1 attributed to the CO and C–OH bonds from carboxyl groups [17], it is reasonable to infer that there are plenty of carboxyl groups on the surface of TA-NCDs-3. Besides that, two peaks of TA-NCDs-3 at 1631 cm−1 and 1263 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CN in the quinone ring and C–N bonds [17].

XPS spectra (Fig. S6) of TA-NCDs exhibit three characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.9 eV and 532.2 eV, which are response for C, N and O elements, respectively. The atomic ratios occupied by each element are displayed in Table S1. The high-resolution C 1s spectra shows that there are five peaks located at 284.6 eV (C–C/CC), 285.3 eV (C–N), 286.5 eV (C–O), 287.8 eV (CO) and 288.7 eV (O–CO) for each TA-NCDs (Fig. 1f and S7a) [18].The fitted N 1s XPS spectra of TA-NCDs exhibit three peaks attributed to pyridinic N located at 399.7 eV, pyrrolic N located at 400.3 eV, and graphitic N located at 401.7 eV (Fig. 1g and S7b) [19,20], illustrating that TA-NCDs are nitrogen-doped and new carbon-nitrogen structures are formed after hydrothermal reaction. The relative abundance of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N are calculated according to the peak areas and atomic ratios (Table 1). It is found that TA-NCDs possess different pyrrolic nitrogen levels. The content of pyrrolic N is increased from 1.28% in TA-NCDs-1 to 2.03% in TA-NCDs-3, and decreased again to 1.21% in TA-NCDs-5. All above results suggest that a series of TA-NCDs with different amount of pyrrolic nitrogen are acquired by varying the ratio of precursors.

The band structure of these TA-NCDs were obtained by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra [21] and cyclic voltammetry (CV) test [22,23]. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra show TA-NCDs have a strong absorption in ultraviolet and visible light regions (Fig. 2a and S8). Corresponding Tauc plots exhibit that TA-NCDs have similar optical band gaps, in which TA-NCDs-3 have three optical band gaps, including 2.08 eV, 3.08 eV and 3.96 eV (Fig. 2b and S8). To clarify the band structure of the TA-NCDs accurately, CV curves were carried out to determine the positions of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of TA-NCDs. It is clearly observed that there are multiple oxidation peaks and reduction peaks for TA-NCDs (Fig. 2c and S9). The HOMO and LUMO energy levels are calculated according to the onset of oxidation and reduction potentials, in which the energy level of ferrocene serve as a reference (Table S2). In order to clarify whether TA-NCDs have a potential in reducing or oxidizing other materials thermodynamically, the electron volt is converted to the electrochemical potential with the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) as the standard reference. As shown in Fig. 2 d and Table S3, the LUMO potentials of TA-NCDs are more negative than the energy level from oxygen to superoxide radicals (
E
O 2
/ ·
O 2 −
= − 0.23
V
), indicating that TA-NCDs are capable to reduce oxygen and produce superoxide radicals [22].

Next, it is demonstrated TA-NCDs possessed oxidase-like catalytic activity. First, the chromogenic substrate of TMB is introduced as the substrate to investigate the oxidase-like activity of TA-NCDs by recording the absorbance at 652 nm of the blue product developed by the oxidized TMB. As shown in Fig. 3a and b and S10, TA-NCDs could oxidize TMB with visible light irradiation and show the optimal oxidase-like activity under 485 nm irradiation, in contrast, the colorimetric reaction of TMB do not proceed in dark. TA-NCDs also show a concentration-dependent effect (Fig. 3 c). The specific activity (SA) of TA-NCDs-3 is 0.452 U/mg (Fig. S11). It is observed that the kinetic of TA-NCDs as a mimic oxidase is well followed the typical Michaelis–Menten equation within the tested concentration range (Fig. 3d). From the Lineweaver–Burk double reciprocal plots, the kinetic parameters including the values of maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis–Menten constant (Km) are obtained and summarized in Fig. S12 and Table S4. In comparison, TA-NCDs-3 exhibit maximal catalytic efficiency than other TA-NCDs as proved by the Vmax values and its Vmax value is 121.95 × 10−8 M/s (Fig. 3e). Notably, TA-NCDs-3 show significantly higher catalytic performance than that of other nanozymes under the same situation, and more than 10 times higher than that of natural enzyme, as indicated by the Vmax values (Table S5).

We also investigated the stability of TA-NCDs in different conditions. Like other nanozymes, the oxidase-like activity of TA-NCDs depend on pH value, with the optimal activity at pH = 4 (Fig. 4 a). TA-NCDs also show the ability of oxidizing TMB in a temperature- and ionic strength-dependent manner. Significantly, TA-NCDs hold excellent oxidase-activity in a broad range of temperature and ionic strengths, even at 4 °C, 85 °C, and in a 1 M NaCl solution (Fig. 4b and c). In addition, the capability of TA-NCDs to oxidize TMB remains stable in various metallic salt solutions, which is only inhibited in Fe3+ and Cu2+ solutions (Fig. 4d).

The catalytic mechanism of TA-NCDs as mimic oxidase was studied in details. Before the tests, the fluorescence spectra experiments demonstrated that TA-NCDs-3 could combine with TMB (Fig. S13). As shown in Fig. 5 a, the colorimetric reaction is clearly enhanced in an O2-saturated atmosphere, and is inhibited in a N2 atmosphere, confirming that the participation of O2 as reactants. Next, active species capture experiments were conducted, in which, EDTA, CuCl2, terephthalic acid (TPA), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and tryptophan (Try) were served as hole, electron, hydroxyl radical, superoxide radicals and singlet oxygen scavengers, respectively. It is found that the addition of CuCl2 reduces the oxidase-like activity of TA-NCDs, in contrast, the addition of EDTA increases their activity (Fig. 5b). The reason is that the capture of electrons impedes the reaction, while the trapping of holes facilitated the use of electrons. The inhibited oxidase-like activity with the addition of SOD demonstrates that superoxide radicals are the intermediate in the catalytic reaction. In addition, the typical triplet signal (1:1:1) assigned to superoxide radicals is merely presented in the ESR spectra via visible light irradiation [22], confirming that the generation of superoxide radical (Fig. 5c and d). These results are consistent with the above conclusion inferred from the band structure of TA-NCDs, that is, TA-NCDs could oxidize TMB through oxygen activation.

Next, in situ TPV experiments were carried out to identify the active sites for this catalytic reaction on the TA-NCDs. The TPV intensity of TA-NCDs under O2 saturated atmosphere (
I
O 2
/ TA − NCDs
) is lower than that under N2 saturated atmosphere (
I
N 2
/ TA − NCDs
), indicating electrons on the surface of TA-NCDs are consumed by oxygen (Fig. 6 a) [22]. However, the above phenomenon is not observed in the presence of excessive TMB (Fig. 6b). The reason is that the active sites are occupied by TMB. In comparison with the TPV data of TA-NCDs, it is clearly seen that the oxygen activation is proportional to pyrrolic nitrogen, suggesting the active sites are pyrrolic nitrogen of TA-NCDs (Fig. 6c, S14 and S15). In addition, the competition for active sites between TMB and oxygen implies that the pyrrolic nitrogen is also the substrate binding sites for TMB. According to the catalytic process and the pyrrolic nitrogen serving as active sites, the rate equation (Eq. (3)) could be deduced as follows:
(Eq. 3)
V =
ν
e −
×
ν
O 2
×
ν
T M B
= k ×
N 3
where V is the catalytic velocity,
ν
e −
,
ν
O 2
, and
ν
T M B
represent the adsorption rate of electron, oxygen, and TMB at the active sites, k and N represent the constant and the number of active sites.

These results suggest pyrrolic nitrogen was critical for the oxidase-like activity, and higher level of pyrrolic nitrogen promote the catalytic activity of TA-NCDs. Next, the correlation equation (Eq. (4)) between the Vmax values and pyrrolic nitrogen content was determined as follows:
(Eq. 4)
V
m a x
= 1.32 ×
10
− 7
[ P y r r o l i c
N ]
3.16
where Vmax represent the maximum catalytic activity and the [Pyrrolic N] is the pyrrolic nitrogen content (Fig. 6d). In a word, the catalysis of TA-NCDs happens on the pyrrolic nitrogen structure, involving the oxygen activation, electron transfer and substrate oxidation (Fig. 6e).

As mentioned above, TA-NCDs possessed oxidase-like activity under visible light irradiation, with a superoxide radicals-mediated catalytic process. As known, superoxide radicals, as one of reactive oxygen radicals, could cause cell damage. Thus, the viability of cells could be estimated the oxidase-like activity of TA-NCDs in cell. Here, the internalization of TA-NCDs in HeLa cells were clarified using confocal microscopy. TA-NCDs show a concentration-, time- and energy-dependent uptake process (Fig. S16, S17 and S18). The fluorescence signals of TA-NCDs are well overlapped with that of mitochondrial dye (MitoTracker® Deep Red FM) but not that of nuclear dye (RedDot™ 1), indicating that TA-NCDs can enrich in mitochondria (Fig. 7 a). Next, the viabilities of cells exposed to TA-NCDs (250 μg/mL) under visible light irradiation are nearly lower 60% than that in dark, implying that TA-NCDs are able to inhibit the growth of HeLa cells (Fig. 7b). As expected, after visible light irradiation, the cellular ROS level of TA-NCDs (250 μg/mL) treated HeLa cells increases about 2.5 folds compared to the non-irradiated group, confirming the biocatalytic activity of TA-NCDs in HeLa cells (Fig. 7c). All these results indicate that TA-NCDs can exert biocatalytic effect as a mimic oxidase in a real cellular environment (Fig. 7d).

CDs with good biocompatibility play a crucial role in nanobiotechnology and nanomedicine fields. However, the exact correlation between the nitrogen-doped form and oxidase-like activity remains unclear, which limits the design and performance optimization of nanozymes. Under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) irradiation, TA-NCDs are able to oxidize TMB and exhibit a catalytic kinetic process like natural enzyme, with optimal Vmax at 121.95 × 10−8 M/s. The catalytic process of TA-NCDs is mediated by superoxide radicals produced from the activation of oxygen. The pyrrolic nitrogen in TA-NCDs serve as the active sites for oxygen activation as well as the substrate binding sites for TMB. Significantly, TA-NCDs could be enriched in the mitochondria of HeLa cells and kill around 60% of HeLa cells upon visible light exposure. Considering that CDs had the characteristics of core-shell structure, there remained much scope for the improvement of CDs as nanozymes by tuning their size, composition, and surface functional groups.

In summary, we fabricated a series of TA-NCDs with mimic oxidase activity, which can be activated by visible light. Multiple experiments had proved that pyrrolic nitrogen of TA-NCDs is the active center, at which oxygen activation and substrate oxidation occurr. TA-NCDs are able to target to mitochondria, produce ROS under light irradiation, and then inhibit the growth of HeLa cells. Our work provide an important understanding on the active structures of carbon-based nanozymes, and open a new way for the design of nanozymes.

Huibo Wang: Data curation, Visualization. Mengling Zhang: Visualization. Kaiqiang Wei: Investigation. Yajie Zhao: Software. Haodong Nie: Software. Yurong Ma: Investigation. Yunjie Zhou: Writing – original draft. Hui Huang: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yang Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mingwang Shao: Writing – review & editing. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, and the 111 Project.
